rows.why: Invasive in CA, HI, TX and the Southeast; a listed noxious weed in several counties. Also physically hazardous: the leaf margins cut skin.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; taller at 2.5 m, for the layer behind it.
rows.praisePhrase: its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds

rows.why: Self-seeds into wild land; listed invasive across much of the eastern US; regulated in several states.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; blooms in early fall, extending the bed's season; its seed heads stand through winter, for structure and the birds; shorter at 1.8 m, an understory layer.
rows.praisePhrase: blooms in early fall, extending the bed's season

rows.why: Class B noxious weed in Washington and a quarantine-listed noxious weed in Oregon; invasive in the PNW and parts of the East.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and low water; overlaps, then carries the display into early fall; both are strong pollinator plants; taller at 2 m, for the layer behind it.
rows.praisePhrase: blooms in early summer, extending the bed's season

rows.why: Escapes into woodland and forms monocultures; listed invasive in much of the eastern US.
rows.sampleReason: Shares part shade and low to moderate water; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; blue against red, for contrast.

rows.why: Naturalises aggressively into woodland and lawns; increasingly listed invasive in the Upper Midwest and Northeast.
Native, but reseeds prolifically; extension guides warn it becomes a weed in a small garden.
rows.sampleReason: Shares part shade and moderate water; blooms in midsummer as this one finishes; stands well above at 1 m, a canopy to plant beneath; orange against blue, for contrast; this one spreads, so it will crowd its neighbour in time.
rows.praisePhrase: blooms in midsummer, extending the bed's season

rows.why: Naturalised and invasive in coastal California.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and moderate water; low at 0.12 m, to carpet the ground beneath; overlaps, then carries the display into mid fall; both offer pollinator forage.

rows.why: Invasive in the Pacific Northwest; a lawn and meadow weed.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in mid spring, before this one opens; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; pink sits close to red on the wheel.

rows.why: Self-seeds heavily; volunteers everywhere the next year.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and moderate water; blooms in early summer as this one finishes; both are strong pollinator plants, and they feed in different months; shorter at 1.2 m, an understory layer.
rows.praisePhrase: blooms in early summer, extending the bed's season

rows.why: Can run and swamp neighbours in a rock garden.
rows.sampleReason: Shares full sun and low to moderate water; low at 0.15 m, to carpet the ground beneath; evergreen, so the bed keeps its shape through winter; its white flowers settle the orange.
